description.task: Demonstrate the difference between DROP TABLE (removes structure + data) and TRUNCATE (removes all data but keeps structure).
instructions: Run CREATE statements, INSERT statements, and the UNION ALL count query.
Run TRUNCATE — verify 0 rows in temp_data.
Re-insert into temp_data — verify it works (table still exists).
Run the information_schema query — only temp_data should appear.
Add a comment explaining: when would you use DROP vs TRUNCATE vs DELETE?
description.hint: DROP = gone forever. TRUNCATE = fast wipe (DDL operation, often not rollback-able). DELETE = removes rows one by one, fully logged, rollback-able. Use TRUNCATE only when you're certain all rows should be removed.
description.expectedOutput:
After TRUNCATE: 0 rows
After DROP: only temp_data in information_schema
Re-insert into temp_data: works normally

description.task: Attempt to DROP a table that has dependent tables (foreign keys), understand the error, and use CASCADE to resolve it.
description.context: When a table is referenced by a FOREIGN KEY in another table, you cannot simply DROP it without first removing the dependency — or using CASCADE.
instructions: Run CREATE and INSERT for original tables, plus the JOIN query.
Drop items first, then categories (Option A).
Recreate with categories2/items2 and run DROP CASCADE (Option B).
Run the information_schema query — both tables should be gone.
In a comment: explain why CASCADE DROP can be dangerous in production.
description.hint: CASCADE drops all dependent objects automatically. In production, this could silently delete entire chains of tables. Always check dependencies before using CASCADE.
description.expectedOutput:
Option A: manual order drop works
Option B: DROP categories2 CASCADE also drops items2

description.task: Write a safe cleanup script that drops tables only if they exist using IF EXISTS, avoiding errors in CI/CD pipelines.
description.context: In automated deployment pipelines, scripts may run even if tables were already cleaned up. Using DROP TABLE IF EXISTS prevents errors when the table doesn't exist.
instructions: Run the entire script from top to bottom.
Observe that DROP IF EXISTS on non-existent tables produces no error.
The second DROP IF EXISTS run should also succeed silently.
In a comment: why is idempotent SQL important in database migration scripts?
description.hint: Idempotent means running the same script multiple times produces the same result without errors. Critical for automated deployments that might retry on failure.
description.expectedOutput:
'Cleanup completed' status
3 rows in orders_backup
'Second cleanup completed' status — no errors
